methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $14M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 23 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 53.2x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Starpath Robotics is a seed-stage company in Space & Aerospace founded by Saurav Shroff and Mihir Gondhalekar. The company has raised $14.5M to date. The Space & Aerospace sector comprises 23 private peers and 6 public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL), though the seed-stage cohort itself remains sparse.

Of three valuation methods examined, only stage-progression proved applicable. This method uses the company's $14.5M total raised as a proxy for current value and applies a 2.5x multiplier typical of seed-to-next-stage advancement, yielding $36M. Peer-median analysis was not executable due to insufficient narrow-peer coverage—while 23 same-sector companies exist, fewer than three matched the target's stage and valuation tier simultaneously. Public-comp anchoring using the sector's median 53.2x price-to-sales multiple could not be applied in the absence of disclosed revenue figures, which is standard for private companies.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $31M to $42M, with a median point of $36M. This range reflects ±15% variance around the stage-progression result, accounting for execution risk inherent at the seed stage. Key uncertainties include the absence of revenue data, limited peer comparability within the seed cohort, and the early-stage burn profile. The valuation depends substantially on the sustainability of typical seed-stage step-up assumptions in the capital-intensive aerospace sector.
